---
# =====================================================================
#  Research highlight — one- to two-page PDF (LaTeX/PDF via Quarto)
#  Canonical layout: see style-guides/research-highlight/README.md
#  Render:  quarto render research-highlights-template.qmd
#  Design system: _style.tex (colours, fonts, boxes, macros)
#  Figure: build with a script in code/ and save to figures/
#
#  LENGTH — use the space the paper warrants; pick ONE budget, stay close:
#    One page  | Background ~120 | Challenge ~180 | Findings ~150 + 1 fig
#    Two pages | Background ~180 | Challenge ~300 | Findings ~250 + 1-2 figs
#  Don't shrink the layout to fit, and don't pad to fill a second page.
# =====================================================================
format:
  pdf:
    pdf-engine: pdflatex
    documentclass: article
    fontsize: 11pt
    geometry:
      - top=0.4in
      - bottom=0.45in
      - left=0.65in
      - right=0.65in
      - includehead
      - headheight=52pt
      - headsep=7pt
      - includefoot
      - footskip=24pt
    include-in-header:
      - file: _style.tex
      - text: |
          % --- Running masthead + footer (logo, label, rule, page number) ---
          \usepackage{fancyhdr}
          \renewcommand{\headrulewidth}{0pt}
          \renewcommand{\footrulewidth}{0pt}
          \fancypagestyle{brief}{%
            \fancyhf{}%
            \fancyhead[L]{%
              \begin{minipage}[c]{0.62\textwidth}\raggedright\color{ink}\includegraphics[height=0.45in]{../logos/eil-logo-maroon.png}\end{minipage}\hfill%
              \begin{minipage}[c]{0.36\textwidth}\raggedleft\fontsize{8.25pt}{10pt}\selectfont\bfseries\color{accentred}\textls[200]{\MakeUppercase{Research Highlight}}\end{minipage}%
              \par\vspace{2pt}%
              \noindent\textcolor{ink}{\rule{\textwidth}{2.2pt}}%
            }%
            \fancyfoot[C]{%
              \color{warmrule}\rule{\textwidth}{0.7pt}\par\vspace{3pt}%
              \hbox to\textwidth{%
                \color{faint}\fontsize{7.4pt}{9pt}\selectfont
                For full paper:\hspace{0.4em}\href{https://doi.org/[DOI]}{\color{faint}\underline{\textls[40]{doi.org/[DOI]}}}%
                \hfill
                Page \thepage%
              }%
            }%
          }
          \pagestyle{brief}
    keep-tex: true
---

```{=latex}
% ---------------------------------------------------------------------
% TITLE + CITATION  (masthead repeats in the page header)
% ---------------------------------------------------------------------
\brieftitle{[Title — state the finding as a claim, not a topic]}
\briefcitation{Lessons from ``[Exact Paper Title]'' by [Authors] in \textit{[Journal]}, [Year].}

\vspace{-2pt}

% override body font size for this document
\fontsize{10.5pt}{13.5pt}\selectfont

% ---------------------------------------------------------------------
% BOTTOM LINE  (~40–50 words)
% ---------------------------------------------------------------------
\begin{bottomline}
{\fontsize{11pt}{15pt}\selectfont
\textbf{[The core finding in one bold sentence.]} [One or two sentences expanding on the finding and why it matters.]
}
\end{bottomline}

\vspace{2pt}

% ---------------------------------------------------------------------
% BACKGROUND  (~120 words, 2 paragraphs)
% ---------------------------------------------------------------------
\sectionhead{Background}
\noindent [Set up the policy problem. Define any key term on first use in \textbf{bold}, then explain it plainly. Spell out agencies on first mention. End on the open question the study tests.]

\noindent [Describe the setting and data in plain language — who was studied, what records the researchers had, and over what period.]

\vspace{2pt}

% ---------------------------------------------------------------------
% THE CHALLENGE  (~180–300 words) — the research-design story
% Head it with the specific obstacle, not a generic label. Narrate the
% comparison as a journey: the naive comparison, why it fails, the next
% instinct, its complication, and how the researchers got around it.
% See style-guides/research-highlight/README.md §3.
% ---------------------------------------------------------------------
\sectionhead{The challenge}
\noindent \textbf{[State the core evaluation problem in one bold sentence a skimmer can catch.]} [Why seeing the counterfactual directly is impossible, so a stand-in comparison is needed.]

\noindent [The naive before/after comparison and why it misleads.]

\noindent [The next instinct — comparing against a different group — and the complication it introduces.]

\noindent [How the researchers got around it. \textbf{Bold the sentence naming the actual comparison they used.} Note what makes it fair.]

\vspace{2pt}

% ---------------------------------------------------------------------
% FINDINGS  (~150 words + figure) — head this with the finding itself,
% not "What the study found."
% FIGURE — one figure, centered. Build via a script in code/ -> figures/
% ---------------------------------------------------------------------
\sectionhead{[The finding, stated — e.g. "No effect on violations or discharges"]}
\noindent [State the result, pointing the reader to the figure below. \textbf{Bold the headline finding.}]

\vspace{4pt}

\begin{center}
\begin{minipage}{0.75\linewidth}
\figtitle{[Sentence-style figure title — what the chart shows]}{Source: [Author(s)] ([Year]), Fig.\ [N].}
\includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{figures/[figure].png}
\par\vspace{3pt}
{\color{faint}\fontsize{7.1pt}{9.5pt}\selectfont \textit{Note:} [Plain-language note: how to read the chart and what to conclude from it.]\par}
\end{minipage}
\end{center}

\vspace{4pt}

\noindent [Short closing paragraph — no subhead. Draw out what the result means, \textbf{bold the one line worth keeping}, and, where relevant, name the limit plainly before restating the point.]

\vspace{4pt}

\begin{moreinfo}
Contact [Corresponding author] ([email]) with questions about this research.\par

[Last, First, and First Last]. [Year]. ``[Exact Paper Title].'' \textit{[Journal]} [vol] ([issue]): [pages]. \href{https://doi.org/[DOI]}{\color{cite}\underline{doi.org/[DOI]}}.\par

\textbf{[First Last]} is [full title and affiliation].\par
\textbf{[First Last]} is [full title and affiliation].\par

The \href{https://www.environmental-inequality-lab.org/}{\color{cite}\underline{Environmental Inequality Lab}} is a nonpartisan research group that applies a rigorous, data-driven approach to understand how our environment shapes economic opportunity and well-being.\par
\end{moreinfo}
```
